coredata.dc:title: Broad-spectrum response NCQDs/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction nanosheets for ciprofloxacin photodegradation: Unraveling the unique roles of NCQDs upon different light irradiation
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Xin
Zhao, Huiping
Liang, Ying
Chen, Fengxi
Li, Jun
Chen, Rong
coredata.dc:description: N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) decorated Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction nanosheets have been successfully constructed by a facile hydrothermal method. The obtained NCQDs/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction exhibits a wide-spectrum absorption ability and remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activities for ciprofloxacin photodegradation from ultraviolet to near-infrared region. The critical roles of NCQDs and two different charge separation and transfer processes of NCQDs/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction under different light irradiations have been elucidated. Upon UV light irradiation, NCQDs act as electron reservoirs and a Z-scheme charge transfer process between Bi2O2CO3 and NCQDs promotes electrons transfer and •O2 − reactive species generation. Under visible and NIR light irradiation, NCQDs act as photosensitizer (hole reservoirs) to harvest solar light and a type-II heterojunction leads to an efficient charge carrier separation and thus high catalytic ability. The mechanisms and pathways of ciprofloxacin degradation driven by different lights are discussed accordingly. This work provides a versatile pathway to well design an efficient wide-spectrum response photocatalyst.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: N-doped carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) decorated Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction nanosheets have been successfully constructed by a facile hydrothermal method. The obtained NCQDs/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction exhibits a wide-spectrum absorption ability and remarkably enhanced photocatalytic activities for ciprofloxacin photodegradation from ultraviolet to near-infrared region. The critical roles of NCQDs and two different charge separation and transfer processes of NCQDs/Bi2O2CO3 heterojunction under different light irradiations have been elucidated. Upon UV light irradiation, NCQDs act as electron reservoirs and a Z-scheme charge transfer process between Bi2O2CO3 and NCQDs promotes electrons transfer and •O2 − reactive species generation. Under visible and NIR light irradiation, NCQDs act as photosensitizer (hole reservoirs) to harvest solar light and a type-II heterojunction leads to an efficient charge carrier separation and thus high catalytic ability. The mechanisms and pathways of ciprofloxacin degradation driven by different lights are discussed accordingly. This work provides a versatile pathway to well design an efficient wide-spectrum response photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:para: Solar-driven photocatalysis has proven to be a promising technique to solve the energy crisis and environmental pollution (Li et al., 2018a; Guo et al., 2019; Di et al., 2019; Liu et al., 2018). The exploration of highly efficient photocatalysts that have a broad spectrum response has become a critical issue to well utilize solar light from ultraviolet to near-infrared region. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon species with size of <10 nm, has attracted countless attention owning to unique properties (Nekoueian et al., 2019; Hu et al., 2019a). Specifically, owing to the size-depended quantum confinement, CQDs have displayed unique optoelectronic properties, including light absorption, up-conversion fluorescence property, photostability, and biocompatibility, which show great value in biological detection, biosensing and catalysis (Lim et al., 2015; Kong et al., 2017; Huang et al., 2017). Light absorption and up-conversion fluorescence property closely related to solar conversion in photocatalytic field. Compared with traditional light harvesting and up-conversion metal materials such as Yb and Tm, nonmetal carbon dots possesses wide light absorption range which is not limited to a certain wavelength region. This unique light harvesting property contributes to the excellent performance of carbon quantum dots in photocatalysis (Wang et al., 2016). In recent years, UV–Visible–NIR light response photocatalysts involved with CQDs have been reported for improving photocatalytic performance in solar conversion systems (Hou et al., 2015; Shi et al., 2017; Yu et al., 2014).

Recently, the application of CQDs have expanded to various fields, and the possible roles of CQDs have also been proposed in many literatures (Zhang et al., 2018a; Moradlou et al., 2018). For examples, Dong demonstrated that the deposition of carbon quantum dots on WO3 led to an improved photocatalytic performance under UV, visible, and NIR light, which contributed to the efficient degradation of RhB (Zhang et al., 2017). Chen found that CQDs modified Bi2WO6 had an enhancement of photodegradation of methylene orange (MO) and bisphenol A (BPA) under full spectrum light (Wang et al., 2018a). Zhu reported various lights (UV, visible, or NIR) could dive organic contaminants degradation over carbon quantum dots/hydrogenated-γ-TaON heterojunction (Hou et al., 2015). The results demonstrated that the CQDs could serve as a photosensitizer to enhance the light capturing ability, especially under NIR light, and thus improving the photocatalytic performances. The excess photons harvested by CQDs could be converted to electrons for producing electricity, however, it cannot explain the promotion of photocatalytic activity, because there are few reports about the photogenerated electrons involved in organic pollution degradation. In other works, Li demonstrated that N-CQDs could improve the photocatalytic activity of Bi4O5I2 for BPA removal by accelerating its surface carriers transfer (Ji et al., 2018). Huang reported that the addition of CQDs could improve the charge separation and transfer efficiency which contributed to the conversion of NO (Huang et al., 2018). Li found that uniformly decorated CQDs could accelerate the charge separation on the surface C3N4 which contributed to the degradation of CBZ (Zhao et al., 2019a). These results strongly suggested that the CQDs could serve as a semiconductor to accelerate charge separation and inhibit charge recombination, which further lead to the promotion of photocatalytic activity. However, there are little detailed explanation about how CQDs accelerate charge separation and inhibit charge recombination. As is well known, Type II and Z-scheme heterojunctions are the most reported heterojunction between two semiconductors, both the two heterojunctions could accelerate charge separation and inhibit charge recombination (Wang et al., 2014). Though in many reports, CQDs were described as a semiconductor to play as a part of Type II or Z-scheme heterojunctions, it was only a possible mechanism provided by authors in a sense, no more data were shown to further confirm which heterojunctions they are in case that the two heterojunctions have the same characterization results. Although there are many reports have demonstrated the improving photocatalytic performance in the presence of CQDs, the essential roles of CQDs are still unclear.

Bi2O2CO3 (BOC), as one of important members of the Aurivillius family, has attracted extensive attention due to its low mammalian toxicity and good photocatalytic capability and stability (Ni et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2015). However, BOC can only utilize UV light of the solar-light spectrum due to its large band-gap energy, which dramatically restrains its practical applications (Huang et al., 2015; Zhang et al., 2019a). In this regard, expanding the light absorption range of BOC is a feasible way to promote the photocatalytic activity. Based on this consideration, various strategies had been developed to optimize the light harvesting properties of BOC aiming at improving the efficiency of photocatalysis. For examples, Xiong employed carbon doping to narrow down the BOC bandgap and promote NO conversion efficiency under visible light (Xiong et al., 2015). Yan found that the deposition of Ag nanoparticle could enhance the light harvesting ability of BOC in visible light region which contributed to the efficient degradation of MO (Peng et al., 2013). Huang reported that the oxygen vacancies concentration tuning could enhance the light absorption ability of BOC during visible region which contributed to hydrogen production, NO removal and tetracycline hydrochloride degradation (Yu et al., 2018). However, those strategies have only realized the utilization of part of visible light, the rest light regions are rarely explored. Light harvesting limitation is an important factor to restrain the practical application of BOC, hence, the exploration of broad-spectrum-response photocatalysts is a vital direction for photocatalytic efficiency promotion.

In this work, a broad-spectrum-driven NCQDs/BOC heterojunction has been constructed via a facile hydrothermal process. The deposition of NCQDs on BOC surface improved the photodegradation efficiency of ciprofloxacin (CIP) under UV light and realized the CIP decomposition upon visible and NIR lights. The main reactive species during CIP degradation under different lights are confirmed by trapping agent measurements and electron spin resonance (ESR) analysis, and the mechanism of CIP degradation are also proposed. The photocatalytic mechanisms of NCQDs/BOC under different light were revealed and the different roles do NCQDs play in NCQDs/BOC were also proposed. In addition, the main degradation pathway of ciprofloxacin was obtained according to the HPLC-MS results, further connections between degradation pathway and reactive species have also been revealed.

Ciprofloxacin (CIP), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Sodium carbonate (Na2CO3), ammonium hydroxide (NH3·H2O), ethanol, isopropyl alcohol (IPA), oxalate (OA), terephthalic acid (TA), and nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co, Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All the reagents were analytical grade without further purification.

NCQDs was synthesized through electrochemical and hydrothermal method. The electrolyte was prepared by mixing ethanol/H2O (84 mL; volume ratio = 20:1) with 1.6 g of NaOH. Graphite rods were used as electrolysis electrode, and electrolytic voltage was set to 20 eV. After 60 min electrolyzing, a black solution was obtained. Then, 5 mL NH3·H2O was added to the solution and heated to 150 °C for 12 h. The obtain solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 20 min to remove precipitate and evaporated under vacuum condition to remove ethanol and water. The obtained concentrated solution was dialyzed 24 h to obtain NCQDs solution, and the NCQDs solution was transferred into −20 °C refrigerators to obtain frozen solution, finally the frozen solution was put into freezer dryer to dehydrate water, NCQDs powder were obtained after 48 h of dehydration.

BOC and NCQDs/BOC were prepared via a facile hydrothermal process. 1.940 g of Bi(NO3)3·5H2O was added to 100 mL mannitol solution (0.1 M) and stirred for 30 min at room temperature. Then, 15 mL saturated Na2CO3 solution was added to the solution and further stirred for 30 min. Finally, the solution was removed into a 150 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave. After being reacted at 150 °C for 12 h, the obtained suspension was centrifuged and washed with distilled water and ethanol for 3 times, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h (BOC). The NCQDs/BOC samples were prepared via the identical process, with the addition of 0.01 g (1 wt%NCQDs/BOC), 0.03 g (3 wt%NCQDs/BOC), 0.05 g (5 wt%NCQDs/BOC), and 0.07 g (7 wt%NCQDs/BOC) of NCQDs into the 100 mL solution before solvothermal reaction, respectively. The synthesis process of NCQDs/BOC was illustrated in Scheme 1 .

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was carried out on Bruker axs D8 Discover (Cu Kα = 1.5406 Å) at a scan rate of 2° min−1 in the 2θ range from 10 to 80°. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) images were recorded on JEOL JSM-2010 transmission electron microscope with 200 kV. X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) data were collected on a VG Multilab 2000 spectrometer using Al Kα (1486.6 eV) radiation as the source. The electron spin resonance (ESR) measurements were performed on a Bruker EMX 10/2.7 ESR spectrometer (X-band). UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) were characterized by a UV–vis spectrometer (Hitachi UH4150), and the Kubelka-Munk method was employed for reflection to absorbance conversion.

Photocatalytic performance of the sample was evaluated by the photodegradation of CIP, using a 240 W Xe lamp (Microsolar300, Beijing Perfectlight) as light source. In a typical experiment, 40 mg photocatalyst was firstly dispersed into 80 mL CIP solution (10 mg/L) and kept magnetically stirring for 1 h in dark to reach an adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The suspension was then exposed to different lights for 60 min. At regular interval, 4 mL suspension was sampled and centrifuged. The CIP absorbance was measured by Mapada spectrophotometer and CIP concentration variation was monitored during the degradation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of intermediates and final products in photocatalysis
ce:section.ce:para: High-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS, Agilent 1260/6310, Ion Trap LC-MS) equipped with an Agilent Model C18 column was used to determine intermediates and final products during CIP photodegradation. The mobile phase is composed of 65% water and 35% acetonitrile (containing 0.2% formic acid), with a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The gradient started with 20% acetonitrile or methanol, which gradually was increased to 30% in 2.5 min and 80% after 3.5 min, and then equilibrated at 4 min and returned to the initial composition within 5 min. The injection volume was 20 μL, and the column temperature was kept at 30 °C. Mass spectrum was scanned in a m/z range from 100 to 450.

Electron spin resonance (ESR) measurement was performed on a JES X-band ESR spectrometer with 5,5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) as a spin-trapping agent. 40 μL DMPO was added to a mixture of sample with water or methanol saturated with air to detect the radicals under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation for 5 min, respectively.

The photoelectrochemical measurements were performed on CHI 660 E workstation (Shanghai, China) and recorded by a standard three-electrode system with platinum foil as the counter electrode, saturated calomel electrode as the reference electrode, and glass carbon as the working electrode (GCE) in 0.5 mol/L Na2SO4 electrolyte solution. Prior to the measurement, 10 mg sample was well-mixed with 0.5 mL DMF and 20 μL Nafion solution (5%, DuPont) and then 40 μL mixture was modified on the GCE surface and dried at room temperature.

Fig. 1 a showed the up-converted photoluminescence spectra of NCQDs solution. With the exciting of different long wavelength light, NCQDs could emit short wavelength (450–550 nm) photoluminescence, confirming the successful synthesis of carbon quantum dots (Lim et al., 2015). As shown in Fig. 1b, the XRD peaks of BOC and NCQDs/BOC samples were well matched with the diffraction patterns of tetragonal phase Bi2O2CO3 (JCPDS: 84–1752), indicative of the fabrication of Bi2O2CO3. No other peak ascribed to NCQDs was detected, which was attributed to high dispersion and the small size of the NCQDs in the NCQDs/BOC composite (Lu et al., 2018a; Zhang et al., 2018b). Fig. 1c showed the UV–Vis absorption spectra of BOC and NCQDs/BOC. Compared with pure BOC, NCQDs/BOC displayed obviously enhanced light absorption in visible and NIR light region, which was consistent with the broad light absorption property of NCQDs, illustrating the successful deposition of NCQDs (Liu et al., 2019; Li et al., 2019).

The microstructure of NCQDs/BOC sample was further characterized by TEM analysis. Fig. 2 a showed the TEM image of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC sample, which revealed the uniform deposition of nanodots on the surface of BOC nanosheet. The HRTEM image (Fig. 2b) showed that the diameter of NCQDs was about 3–7 nm, further illustrating the successful loading of NCQDs on Bi2O2CO3. HAADF-STEM image indicated the solid nanosheet structure of Bi2O2CO3 with the thickness of about 20 nm (Fig. 2c). The corresponding element mapping image demonstrated the existence of N and the uniform distribution of three elements (Bi, O and N) in 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC.

In order to further make clear of the deposition information of NCQDs, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurement was performed to analyze the surface element of BOC and NCQDs/BOC. The full XPS spectra indicated that both BOC and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC mainly consisted of Bi, C and O element, and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC displayed a weak peak of N1s (Figure S1, Supporting Information). In the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 3 ), Bi 4f peak at 159.0 eV and 164.3 eV resulted from the vibration of Bi 4f7/2 and Bi 4f5/2. The O 1s peak was observed at 530.7 and 532.0 eV due to the Bi–O and C–O vibrations, respectively (Wang et al., 2018a; Ji et al., 2018). The C 1s peak located at 288.4 and 284.6 eV originated from the vibration of OC–O and C–C (adventitious carbon), respectively (Liu et al., 2019; Sun et al., 2017; Zhou et al., 2016), and the peak at 286.1 eV corresponded to the C–N vibration (Wang et al., 2017), which indicated the successful introduction of NCQDs. Compared with peaks of BOC, the Bi 4f, Bi–O and C–O peaks of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed an up-shift binding energy, which might be ascribed to the interaction between BOC and NCQDs (Wang et al., 2019). The peak at 286.1 eV in 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC was attributed to the C–N vibration. N 1s peaks appeared at 400.0 and 401.4 eV as a consequence of the vibration of C–NC and N–H (Zhang et al., 2019b), which suggested the presence of nitrogen element in 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC and further verified the formation of NCQDs/BOC herterostructures. In addition, we also studied the structures and compositions of pure NCQDs. It is found that N 1s peaks showed a slightly down-shift of 0.2–0.5 eV compared with that of NCQDs/BOC heterojunction (Fig. 3e) and C1s peak at 288.6, 286.0 and 288.1 eV were observed (Fig. 3f) from a high-resolution XPS spectra (Li et al., 2018b). Base on above results, it could be concluded that the nitrogen element in 5 wt%NCQDs/BOC derived from NCQDs, which could further confirm the successful deposition of NCQDs on the surface of BOC.

Ciprofloxacin (CIP) was chosen as the target pollutant to determine the photocatalytic activities of the prepared samples under different light irradiation. As shown in Fig. 4 a, BOC showed efficient degradation of CIP under UV–Vis light irradiation, which indicated that 50% of CIP could be degraded within 60 min. However, BOC showed very limited degradation of CIP under visible light, only 9% of CIP could be degraded within 120 min (Fig. 4b) and almost no CIP could be degraded under NIR light irradiation. After the modification of NCQDs, NCQDs/BOC showed an obvious efficiency improvement of CIP degradation under UV–Vis, visible light irradiation and even reached efficient degradation toward CIP under NIR light irradiation (Fig. 4c). The corresponding linear plots of -ln (Ct/C0) versus degradation time under different light were carried out. 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed highest efficiency of CIP degradation under UV–Vis, visible and NIR light irradiation after the deposition of NCQDs (Figure S2a, 2b and 2c, Supporting Information). Compared with pure BOC, 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed 2.7, 25.9 and 85.7 times higher CIP degradation efficiency under UV–Vis, visible and NIR light irradiation, respectively (Fig. 4d). The XRD pattern of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC after five times cycle was almost the same with the XRD patterns of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC, indicating the good stability of samples in photocatalysis (Figure S2d, Supporting Information). The promotion of degradation efficiency might be attributed to the light absorption and up-conversion property of NCQDs (Wang et al., 2018b). It is found that 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed 45.8% TOC removal efficiency with 91.1% degradation efficiency of CIP, which is higher than the TOC removal efficiency of BOC (18.7%) with 50.2% degradation efficiency of CIP under UV–Vis light irradiation (Figure S3a, Supporting Information), and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed 26.7% and 8.6% TOC removal efficiency under visible and NIR light irradiation, respectively (Figure S3b and S3c, Supporting Information).

Reactive species have close relationship with the removal of contaminants (Guo et al., 2020; Cheng et al., 2020; Lu et al., 2018b). In order to explore the mechanism of CIP degradation, the trapping agent experiment was performed to explore the roles did reactive oxygen species play in. N2, isopropanol (IPA), oxalate (OA) were added to as •O2 −, •OH and hole scavengers, respectively. As shown in Fig. 4e, the CIP degradation rate of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC decreased from 91.1% to 23.6%, 64.3% and 37.9% respectively in the present of N2, IPA and OA, indicating that •O2 −, •OH and hole were all participate in the degradation of CIP under UV–Vis light irradiation. CIP degradation rate of BOC decreased from 50.2% to 47.8%, 26.7% and 18.3% respectively in the present of N2, IPA and OA, indicating that •OH and hole were participated in the CIP degradation of BOC under UV–Vis light irradiation while O2 − didn’t contribute to the CIP degradation of BOC under UV–Vis light irradiation. The trapping agent experiment was also performed to explore the roles did reactive oxygen species play in the CIP degradation of 5 wt%NCQDs/BOC under visible light and NIR light (Fig. 4f). CIP degradation rate of 5 wt%NCQDs/BOC only decreased in the present of OA, almost showed no decrease in the present of N2 and IPA, indicating that hole contributed the CIP degradation with 5 wt%NCQDs/BOC under visible and NIR light.

We used ESR spectra to detect the reactive species generated by exposure BOC and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC upon different light irradiation. Fig. 5 a and b showed the ESR spectra over BOC and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC under UV–visible light. No peak was detected in the dark for both BOC and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC. The characteristic peaks of -•O2 − and -•OH signals were observed in the presence of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC, while BOC displayed no •O2 − signal and a weak •OH signal under the identical irradiation condition, which indicated that 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC had stronger ability for •O2 − and •OH radicals generation under UV–visible light irradiation compared with pure BOC. Fig. 5c and d displayed the ESR spectra obtained under NIR light over BOC and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC. No radical signal was detected for both 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC and BOC, revealing no radicals generation under NIR light. To further confirm the type of reactive species, molecular probe experiments were carried out (Bariki et al., 2020). As shown in Figure S4a, 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed efficient reaction rate for nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) transformation under UV–Vis light, indicating that 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC was favorable to the generation of •O2 − while BOC showed almost no reaction with NBT. As shown in Figure S4b, 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed higher TA-NaOH fluorescence spectra signal than BOC under UV–Vis light, indicating that 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC had a stronger ability of producing •OH than BOC, which was consistent with the ESR results. These results were all consistent with the previous trapping agent experiment.

To make clear the mechanism of CIP degradation efficiency promotion via NCQDs deposition, the band structure of BOC was constructed to help understand the process of photocatalyst. Mott-Schottky plot was applied to locate conduction band (ECB) of BOC. As shown in Figure S5a (Supporting Information), the positive slope of curve suggested that BOC is n-type semiconductor, which is consistent with the literature reports (Jia et al., 2019). The flat band potential (Efb) of BOC is located at −0.06 V vs. SCE, which corresponds to 0.18 V vs NHE. The Efb of the n-type semiconductor may be considered as the conduction band position (Zhu et al., 2019). The band gap of BOC is 3.36eV (Figure S5b, Supporting Information), therefore the ECB and EVB of BOC are estimated to be 0.18 and 3.54 V vs. NHE, respectively. The conduction band edge (ECB) and valence band position (EVB) of BOC could be also predicted by the following equation:
E VB

ECB and EVB were the conduction band edge and valence band positive of BOC respectively. χ was the absolute electronegativity of the semiconductor, Ee was the energy of free electrons on the hydrogen scale (4.50 eV), and Eg was the band gap of the BOC (3.36 eV, Figure S5b, Supporting Information). According to the equation, the ECB and EVB values of BOC were calculated to be 0.36 and 3.72 eV, respectively.

Based on the band structure of BOC, combined with the reactive oxygen species generation and trapping agent experiment of BOC and 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC, two heterojunctions photocatalysts and two pathways of electron transfer between NCQDs and BOC under different light were proposed. NCQDs was not only a nanomaterial but also a semiconductor which owned band structures, therefore NCQDs could serve as a part in heterojunction semiconductor materials. As shown in Scheme 2 , the conduction band edge of BOC was 0.36 V, which was positive than the potential of molecular oxygen activation (O
2
/·O
2
− =-0.33 V), which confirmed that BOC was unable to activate molecular oxygen to generate ·O2 − (Ding et al., 2017). However, 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC could generate·O2 − under UV–Vis light irradiation, so the·O2 − might be generated by the NCQDs under UV–Vis light. Based on the above analysis, a direct Z-scheme heterojunction under UV–Vis light was proposed: NCQDs and BOC all could be activated under UV–Vis light, NCQDs acted as electron reservoirs to activate molecular oxygen by electrons on conduction band, the holes on valence band recombined with electrons on conduction band of BOC to accelerate charge transfer between NCQDs and BOC, the hole in the valence band of BOC would react with H2O to generate ·OH. Based on the calculated bandgap, BOC could not be excited by visible and NIR light owing to its wide bandgap. Therefore, the light absorption of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC under visible and NIR light was completed ascribed to the light absorption of NCQDs. According to the previous trapping agent experiments and ESR results, hole was the main reactive oxygen species in the CIP degradation of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC under visible and NIR light while no •O2 − and •OH produced in the same condition. Based on these analyses, a type II photocatalyst under visible and NIR light was proposed. BOC could not be excited under visible and NIR light, and the activated NCQDs served as a photosensitizer to harvest solar light. The photogenerated electrons in the conduction band of NCQDs was not able to active O2, thus transferred to the more positive conduction band of BOC. The conduction band potential of BOC was not negative enough to generate·O2 −. The valence band potential of NCQDs was negative than the water oxidation potential, so the holes in valence band of BOC could not active H2O to generate ·OH, therefore NCQDs played as hole reservoirs in the degradation process in a sense.

The surface charge density was performed to characterize the interfacial charge transfer between NCQDs and BOC. Fig. 6 a and Fig. 6b showed the photocurrent density of BOC and 5 wt%NCQDs/BOC under UV–Vis and NIR light, respectively. Compared with other photocatalysts, 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC showed highest signal of photocurrent density under UV–Vis light and NIR light irradiation, indicating the highest efficiency of electron and hole separation. The excessive deposition of NCQDs may inhibited the separation of electrons and holes, and retarded the transfer of charge carriers, resulting in the decrease of photocatalytic activity of 7 wt% NCQDs/BOC. The Nyquist plot diameter (the impedance) results (Figure S6, Supporting Information) also verified this conclusion. The impedance decreased gradually with the content of NCQDs and then increased. In Scheme 2, two different roles of NCQDs over the different heterojunctions under UV, visible and NIR light irradiation were proposed. The enhanced photocurrent density was the attributed to the NCQDs deposition. Fig. 6c showed the electrons transfer between NCQDs and BOC with NCQDs deposition. With the deposition of NCQDs, more electron and hole reservoirs generated, and the process of electrons transfer from BOC to NCQDs under UV light irradiation was accelerated, also promoting the process of electrons transfer from NCQDs to BOC under visible and NIR light irradiation.

HPLC-MS spectra was used to understand the CIP photodegradation pathway. The MS spectra of the intermediates during CIP degradation were listed in Figure S7 and Figure S8 (Supporting Information), and the proposed structural formulas of the major fragment ions in MS were listed in Table S1 (Supporting Information). Based on the data analysis and literatures (Xu et al., 2019; Zhang et al., 2018c; Wen et al., 2018), three degradation pathways of CIP under UV–Vis light over 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC were proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 7 a, the main process of pathway I was the removal of cyclopropane. The activation of piperazine ring was replacing hydrogen atom with hydroxyl radial, which resulted in the activation of carbon-nitrogen band. The main process of pathway II was the piperazine ring removal and pyrrole ketone ring opening. The activation of piperazine ring was replacing hydrogen atom with active oxygen, which resulted in the activation of piperazine ring and final removal. The activation of pyrrole ketone ring was replacing hydrogen atom and carboxyl with active oxygen and hydroxyl radical, respectively, which resulted in the activation of pyrrole ketone ring and ring open process. The main process of pathway III was the piperazine ring destruction. The activation of piperazine ring was replacing hydrogen atom with active oxygen, which resulted in the destruction of piperazine ring. Two degradation pathways of CIP under NIR light by 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC were proposed. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the main process of pathway IV was the removal of cyclopropane which was similar with the pathway I of CIP degradation. The main process of pathway V was the piperazine ring destruction which was consistent with the pathway III of CIP degradation.

Based on above results, it could be concluded that the pathways of CIP degradation were different under different light irradiation in the presence of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC. According to the literature report, different pathways of CIP degradation had closely related to the generation of reactive species (Hu et al., 2019b; Ou et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2015; Zhao et al., 2019b; Tang et al., 2019). Pathway I, pathway II and pathway III were the main pathways of CIP degradation which were driven by reactive species of •O2 −, •OH and h+ in the presence of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC under UV–Vis light, pathway IV and pathway V were the main pathways of CIP degradation which were driven by reactive species of h+ in the presence of 5 wt% NCQDs/BOC under visible and NIR light. By establishing the connection between destruction of functional group (degradation pathways) and reactive species, it might be concluded that the destruction of cyclopropane (pathway I and pathway IV) and piperazine ring (pathway III and pathway V) had closely related to the generation of h+, the destruction of pyrrole ketone ring (pathway II) had closely related to the generation of •O2 − and •OH.

In this work, a novel broad-spectrum response NCQDs/BOC heterojunction photocatalyst has been synthesized through a facile thermal treatment. NCQDs/BOC realizes the efficient degradation of CIP under visible and NIR light through the decoration of NCQDs. More importantly, the different roles of NCQDs for CIP degradation under UV and Vis-NIR light are proposed. Z-Scheme electrons transfer pathway appears in the presence of NCQDs/BOC under UV light, and NCQDs play as electron reservoirs which could promote the production of superoxide radical and hydroxyl radical closely related to the destruction of pyrrole ketone ring. Type II electrons transfer pathway appears under visible and NIR light, NCQDs not only play as photosensitizer which could harvest visible light to produce electrons but also serve as hole reservoirs to produce holes, which are closely related to the destruction of cyclopropane and piperazine ring. This work not only realizes the utilization of NIR light for wide band gap semiconductor BOC, but also reveals the essential roles of NCQDs under different light irradiation. The relationships between pathways of electrons transfer, generation of reactive species and destruction of functional group of ciprofloxacin in NCQDs/BOC are also elucidated.

Xin Hu: Writing - original draft, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Huiping Zhao: Writing - review & editing, Validation. Ying Liang: Writing - review & editing, Validation. Fengxi Chen: Writing - review & editing, Validation. Jun Li: Writing - review & editing. Rong Chen: Writing - review & editing, Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Supervision.
